questions.question: Explain the conditions required for similarity of two triangles.
questions.answer: Two triangles are similar if their corresponding angles are equal and their corresponding sides are proportional.

questions.question: Explain the Basic Proportionality Theorem with reasoning.
questions.answer: The theorem states that a line drawn parallel to one side of a triangle divides the other two sides in the same ratio, establishing proportionality.

questions.question: Explain the AAA similarity criterion and its significance.
questions.answer: AAA similarity states that if all corresponding angles of two triangles are equal, then the triangles are similar, ensuring same shape.

questions.question: Explain why AA similarity criterion is sufficient for triangle similarity.
questions.answer: If two angles are equal, the third angle automatically becomes equal due to angle sum property, ensuring similarity.

questions.question: Explain the SSS similarity criterion with explanation.
questions.answer: SSS similarity states that if corresponding sides of two triangles are proportional, their angles become equal and triangles are similar.

questions.question: Explain the SAS similarity criterion in detail.
questions.answer: If one angle of a triangle is equal to one angle of another triangle and the including sides are proportional, the triangles are similar.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between scale factor and corresponding sides.
questions.answer: Scale factor represents the ratio in which corresponding sides of similar triangles increase or decrease.

questions.question: Explain why corresponding angles remain equal in similar triangles.
questions.answer: Similarity preserves shape, and shape preservation ensures equality of corresponding angles.

questions.question: Explain why corresponding sides are proportional in similar triangles.
questions.answer: Similar triangles are scaled versions of each other, hence sides change in the same ratio.

questions.question: Explain why a square and a rhombus are not similar.
questions.answer: Though their sides may be proportional, their corresponding angles are not equal.

questions.question: Explain how parallel lines help in proving similarity.
questions.answer: Parallel lines create equal angles and proportional sides, helping establish triangle similarity.

questions.question: Explain indirect measurement using similarity.
questions.answer: Indirect measurement uses similar triangles to calculate heights and distances not directly measurable.

questions.question: Explain why all equiangular triangles are similar.
questions.answer: Equiangular triangles have equal corresponding angles, which implies proportional sides.

questions.question: Explain the importance of correspondence in triangle similarity.
questions.answer: Correct correspondence ensures correct comparison of sides and angles between triangles.

questions.question: Explain the relationship between areas of similar triangles.
questions.answer: The ratio of areas of similar triangles is equal to the square of the ratio of their corresponding sides.

questions.question: Explain why similar triangles are not always congruent.
questions.answer: Similar triangles may differ in size even though their shapes are identical.

questions.question: Explain how medians behave in similar triangles.
questions.answer: Medians of similar triangles are proportional to their corresponding sides.

questions.question: Explain the role of similarity in map reading.
questions.answer: Maps use similarity to represent large distances on a reduced scale accurately.

questions.question: Explain why correct order of vertices is important in similarity statements.
questions.answer: Correct order ensures accurate correspondence of sides and angles.

questions.question: Explain how similarity is used in surveying.
questions.answer: Surveying uses similar triangles to calculate inaccessible distances.

questions.question: Explain why all circles are similar but all polygons are not.
questions.answer: Circles always maintain shape, while polygons require both angle and side proportionality.

questions.question: Explain why side proportionality alone is not enough for polygon similarity.
questions.answer: Angles may differ even if side ratios are equal, so shape may change.

questions.question: Explain why angle equality alone is not sufficient for polygon similarity.
questions.answer: Even with equal angles, sides may not be proportional, changing the shape.

questions.question: Explain similarity as a geometric transformation.
questions.answer: Similarity enlarges or reduces figures while preserving shape and angle measures.

questions.question: Explain why triangle similarity is fundamental in geometry.
questions.answer: Triangle similarity forms the basis for proofs, constructions, and applications.
